content.problemStatement:
**This problem has subtasks based on different values of NNN**.

A polygon is said to have KKK intersections if exactly KKK unordered pairs of its edges properly intersect. A pair of edges of a polygon is said to properly intersect if they are **not parallel** to each other, and intersect at a point that is **not an endpoint** of either edge.

You will be given an integer NNN, and your task is to print a polygon with exactly KKK self-intersections for K=0,1,2,‚Ä¶,NK = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, NK=0,1,2,‚Ä¶,N. Each polygon can have at most 150150150 vertices and integer coordinates in the range [0,104][0, 10^4][0,104]. Further, the polygon should not contain the same point twice.

**Note:** The polygon is allowed to have collinear points.

You are provided with a checker for this task, that you can run locally on your output file, and it will either print that all checks were passed, or it will print the condition that was not satisfied. You can access the checker at Google Drive or Dropbox.

To run this checker, you need to first compile it with

`g++ checker_polygon.cpp -o checker_polygon`

and then run it with

`./checker_polygon output_file.txt N`

replacing NNN with your actual value of NNN for the specific test case.


Input Format

The only line of input contains a single integer NNN.


Output Format
For each test case, output N+1N + 1N+1 polygons. For the (000-indexed) KKK-th polygon, the number of pairs of edges that properly intersect must be exactly KKK.


To print each polygon, do the following:



first, print NiN_iNi‚Äã (3‚â§Ni‚â§150)3 \le N_i \le 150)3‚â§Ni‚Äã‚â§150), the number of vertices in this polygon.
Next, print NiN_iNi‚Äã lines, each containing a pair of integers, (xj,yj)(x_j, y_j)(xj‚Äã,yj‚Äã) (0‚â§xj,yj‚â§1040 \le x_j, y_j \le 10^40‚â§xj‚Äã,yj‚Äã‚â§104), representing the vertices in order. The vertices must be distinct.
We will assume the polygon is constructed by adding an edge between (xj,yj)(x_j, y_j)(xj‚Äã,yj‚Äã) and (xj+1,yj+1)(x_{j + 1}, y_{j + 1})(xj+1‚Äã,yj+1‚Äã) for every 1‚â§j‚â§Ni1 \le j \le N_i1‚â§j‚â§Ni‚Äã, treating (xNi+1,yNi+1)(x_{N_i + 1}, y_{N_i + 1})(xNi‚Äã+1‚Äã,yNi‚Äã+1‚Äã) as (x1,y1)(x_1, y_1)(x1‚Äã,y1‚Äã).
